```
[35m@@ -0,0 +1,90 @@[m
[32m+[m[32m# Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.[m
[32m+[m[32m#[m

[32m+[m[32m# by the Apache License, Version 2.0.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Regression test: INSERT INTO ... SELECT ... cannot transitively refer to sources.[m
[32m+[m[32m# The selection may refer to views/materialized views, but the transitive[m
[32m+[m[32m# dependencies must not include sources or source-export tables.[m
[32m+[m[32m#[m
[32m+[m[32m# Simple (employee, salary, type) example to reproduce the error.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mmode cockroach[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Set up a source and a table that depends on it[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE SOURCE emp_src FROM LOAD GENERATOR COUNTER[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE TABLE employees_from_src FROM SOURCE emp_src[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# View over source-export table (transitively depends on source)[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE VIEW emp_view AS SELECT counter AS salary, 'full_time'::text AS type FROM employees_from_src[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Plain user table (target of INSERT)[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE TABLE salary_exceptions (employee text, salary int, type text)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# INSERT INTO ... SELECT ... from view that transitively refers to source -&gt; error[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement error invalid selection[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT 'Alice', salary, type FROM emp_view LIMIT 1[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Same error when selecting directly from source-export table[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement error invalid selection[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT 'Bob', counter, 'part_time' FROM employees_from_src LIMIT 1[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Same error when selecting directly from the source. Use a derived table to[m
[32m+[m[32m# alias the single column from emp_src (SELECT * works; column name may not).[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement error invalid selection[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT 'Dave', f1::int, 'contractor' FROM (SELECT * FROM emp_src LIMIT 1) AS t(f1)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Set up a plain old table and do the same thing, it should now work[m
[32m+[m[32m# Valid: INSERT INTO ... SELECT ... from plain table only[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE TABLE plain_employees (employee text, salary int, type text)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO plain_employees VALUES ('Carol', 100, 'full_time')[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT employee, salary, type FROM plain_employees[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Set up a basic view over a plain table, and do the same thing (insert)[m
[32m+[m[32m# Valid: INSERT INTO ... SELECT ... from view over plain table (no source in chain)[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE VIEW plain_emp_view AS SELECT employee, salary, type FROM plain_employees[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO plain_employees VALUES ('Eve', 200, 'part_time')[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT employee, salary, type FROM plain_emp_view WHERE employee = 'Eve'[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m# Set up a materialized view over a plain table, and do the same thing (insert)[m
[32m+[m[32m# Valid: INSERT INTO ... SELECT ... from materialized view over plain table (no source in chain)[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mCREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW plain_emp_mv AS SELECT employee, salary, type FROM plain_employees[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO plain_employees VALUES ('Frank', 300, 'contractor')[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mstatement ok[m
[32m+[m[32mINSERT INTO salary_exceptions (employee, salary, type)[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT employee, salary, type FROM plain_emp_mv WHERE employee = 'Frank'[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mquery TIT rowsort[m
[32m+[m[32mSELECT * FROM salary_exceptions[m
[32m+[m[32m----[m
[32m+[m[32mCarol 100 full_time[m
[32m+[m[32mEve 200 part_time[m
[32m+[m[32mFrank 300 contractor[m

```

I have the full diff. I will analyze it and run the local checks. I notice a few things from the diff:

1. **error.rs** - There's a typo in the Display impl: `"invalid selection: operation may only refer to user-defined tables.; \` - there's a semicolon before the backslash that shouldn't be there (style says "no trailing punctuation" for primary message). Also ".;" looks wrong - probably should be ". " or just ". " for the next sentence.

2. I need to run: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy (scoped to adapter maybe since that's what changed), bin/lint.

3. I need to find all usages of `InvalidTableMutationSelection` to ensure the new variant is handled everywhere.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

1. cargo fmt failed because rustup isn't configured (no default toolchain). I'll note that in the review - the author would run it in their env.
2. bin/lint failed because shellcheck isn't installed - that's an environment thing, not related to the diff. I'll note it.
3. InvalidTableMutationSelection is only used in error.rs (definition + Display + sql_state) and inner.rs (construction). So the new variant is fully wired.

I will look at the error message again in the diff. In error.rs:
```
write!(
    f,
    "invalid selection: operation may only refer to user-defined tables.; \
     {object_type} '{}' may not be used in this operation; \
     the selection may refer to views and materialized views, but transitive \
     dependencies must not include sources or source-export tables",
    object_name.quoted()
)
```
Issues:
- "tables.;" has a semicolon - style says primary message: lowercase first letter, no trailing punctuation. So "tables." is correct but the semicolon makes it "tables.;" which is wrong.
- Actually looking at the style guide again: "Primary: short, factual, lowercase first letter, no trailing punctuation. Detail/hint: complete sentences, capitalized, period." So the first part could end with a period. But "tables.;" has an extra semicolon - that's a typo.
- The second part "{object_type} '{}' may not be used..." - the object_name is passed to quoted(), so we get something like "source 'emp_src'" - good. But the format string has " '{}' " and one argument object_name.quoted() - so we're missing the object_type in the format! I will look again: write!(f, "... {object_type} '{}' ...", object_name.quoted()) - so we have two format args: {object_type} and {}. So the arguments should be object_type and object_name.quoted(). So we have "object_type" in the string and one "{}" - so we need two arguments: object_type, object_name.quoted(). So the current code only passes object_name.quoted() - that's a bug! The {object_type} would be filled but we have only one {} for the quoted name, and we're only passing one argument. So we need write!(f, "...", object_type, object_name.quoted()). I will check - in the diff we have:
  "invalid selection: operation may only refer to user-defined tables.; \"
  " {object_type} '{}' may not be used in this operation; \"
  ...
  object_name.quoted()
So there are two format parameters: {object_type} and {}. So we need two arguments. The code only has one argument (object_name.quoted()). So we need to add object_type as the first argument. That's a bug - at runtime this would print the second sentence with wrong ordering or panic. Actually in Rust, write! would use object_name.quoted() for the first {} (which is {object_type} - no wait, {object_type} is a named parameter, and {} is positional. So the argument object_name.quoted() goes to the first positional {}. So we get object_type from the name, and the single {} gets object_name.quoted(). So the output would be: "invalid selection: ... tables.; source 'emp_src' may not be used..." - so actually it's correct! We have {object_type} (named) and {} (takes first positional = object_name.quoted()). So only the semicolon typo "tables.;" needs fixing.

I will read the error.rs file to see the exact code.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So the format string has:
- `{object_type}` - this is a named format parameter, so it gets its value from the variable object_type in scope (from the match arm).
- `'{}'` - this is a positional format, and we have one argument `object_name.quoted()`. So the `{}` gets the quoted object name.

So the output would be: "... tables.; source 'emp_src' may not be used..." - correct. The typo is "tables.;" - should be "tables." (or "tables. " for space before the next part). Style says "no trailing punctuation" for primary - so "tables." might be intended. The semicolon is clearly wrong.

I will also verify: in inner.rs we need to make sure the types used (Source, Secret, etc.) are the right enum variants. I will check - they use Source, Secret, Connection, Table, View, MaterializedView, ContinualTask. These need to be from the right type - likely CatalogItemType or similar.
</details></think>
